articles.title: OpenAI Codex CLI `0.140.0` landed June 15 with `/usage`, better `/goal` preservation, `/import` from Cla…
articles.summary: OpenAI Codex CLI `0.140.0` landed June 15 with `/usage`, better `/goal` preservation, `/import` from Claude Code, encrypted CLI/MCP OAuth credential storage, SQLite recovery, and MCP startup/auth fixes. Source: https://developers.openai.com/codex/changelog and https://github.com/openai/codex/releases/tag/rust-v0.140.0 — credible because OpenAI docs + official repo release; materially new vs yesterday’s `0.139.0` item.
articles.emailSummary: OpenAI Codex CLI `0.140.0` landed June 15 with `/usage`, better `/goal` preservation, `/import` from Claude Code, encrypted CLI/MCP OAuth creden…
articles.whyItMatters: This is one of the vetted AI signals from Dustin’s Daily AI Briefing, split into its own card so the morning scan covers multiple AI developments instead of one giant article.

glossary.definition: Model Context Protocol: a standard way for AI tools to connect to outside apps, files, and services.

learningPage.title: What this means
learningPage.explanationText: This is a developer-tooling AI story: the update affects how coding agents inspect apps, connect tools, or run inside a developer workflow. The teaching angle is integration. The more an AI coding tool connects to browsers, terminals, and external services, the more useful — and riskier — it becomes. The main terms to understand are MCP, CLI, AI capability, model, guardrail, vendor dependency. Read for what is newly available, whether it is preview or production-ready, what systems it connects to, and any limits around pricing, permissions, or rate limits.

glossary.definition: Model Context Protocol: a standard way for AI tools to connect to outside apps, files, and services.
glossary.whyItMatters: MCP matters because it is becoming a common plug shape for connecting AI agents to real tools, files, and services.

glossary.definition: Command-line interface: a text-based tool run from a terminal.
glossary.whyItMatters: CLI support matters when AI tools need to fit into developer and automation workflows instead of only a web chat.

glossary.definition: The specific new thing an AI system can do, such as use a browser, run code, connect tools, or access a model.
glossary.whyItMatters: AI news is easier to judge when you identify the exact capability instead of treating every update as general hype.

glossary.definition: The AI system doing the predicting or writing, like Claude, GPT, or Gemini.
glossary.whyItMatters: Model choice affects cost, quality, speed, policy limits, and whether a workflow can keep running when access changes.

glossary.definition: A rule, limit, approval step, or monitoring system meant to keep AI behavior safe and controlled.
glossary.whyItMatters: Guardrails determine whether a tool is safe enough for real work, especially when it touches business systems.

glossary.definition: Reliance on one company, model, or platform for an important workflow.
glossary.whyItMatters: If access, pricing, or policy changes, dependency can suddenly become an operational risk.

articles.title: NVIDIA’s open-source `SkillSpector` is a security scanner for AI agent skills, with no-LLM scanning, JSO…
articles.summary: NVIDIA’s open-source `SkillSpector` is a security scanner for AI agent skills, with no-LLM scanning, JSON/Markdown/SARIF output, and patterns for prompt injection, exfiltration, privilege escalation, MCP abuse, tool misuse, and malicious scripts. Source: https://github.com/NVIDIA/SkillSpector — credible enough for tool-spotting because it is NVIDIA’s repo; verify before trusting, but it maps directly to Hermes/Codex/Claude skills.

articles.whyItMatters: This is one of the vetted AI signals from Dustin’s Daily AI Briefing, split into its own card so the morning scan covers multiple AI developments instead of one giant article.

glossary.definition: An AI helper that can take steps, use tools, or follow a workflow instead of only producing one answer.
Model Context Protocol: a standard way for AI tools to connect to outside apps, files, and services.

learningPage.title: What this means
learningPage.explanationText: This is an AI-agent workflow story: AI is being used to help define, run, or supervise a sequence of work instead of only chatting. The teaching angle is control. Once AI can trigger workflows, the key concepts are permissions, sandboxing, auditability, and whether humans can understand what it changed. The main terms to understand are Markdown workflow, MCP, agent, AI capability, model, guardrail. Read for the exact job the agent can do, what tools it touches, what permissions it starts with, and what safety controls are named in the story.

glossary.definition: A text-file workflow written in a simple document format instead of a traditional programming language.
glossary.whyItMatters: This lowers the barrier to creating automation, but also means non-programmers may create powerful workflows that need guardrails.

glossary.definition: Model Context Protocol: a standard way for AI tools to connect to outside apps, files, and services.
glossary.whyItMatters: MCP matters because it is becoming a common plug shape for connecting AI agents to real tools, files, and services.

glossary.definition: An AI helper that can take steps, use tools, or follow a workflow instead of only producing one answer.
glossary.whyItMatters: This term appears in the article, so understanding it helps you read the source without getting lost in jargon.

glossary.definition: The specific new thing an AI system can do, such as use a browser, run code, connect tools, or access a model.
glossary.whyItMatters: AI news is easier to judge when you identify the exact capability instead of treating every update as general hype.

glossary.definition: The AI system doing the predicting or writing, like Claude, GPT, or Gemini.
glossary.whyItMatters: Model choice affects cost, quality, speed, policy limits, and whether a workflow can keep running when access changes.

glossary.definition: A rule, limit, approval step, or monitoring system meant to keep AI behavior safe and controlled.
glossary.whyItMatters: Guardrails determine whether a tool is safe enough for real work, especially when it touches business systems.

articles.title: Google’s Gemini API changelog says Imagen 4/Gemini 3 image models shut down Aug. 17, and Veo 2/3 models…
articles.summary: Google’s Gemini API changelog says Imagen 4/Gemini 3 image models shut down Aug. 17, and Veo 2/3 models shut down June 30, with migration guidance to newer Veo 3.1 models. Source: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/changelog — credible because it is Google’s official API release notes; practical only if any media workflow still uses those model IDs.

briefs.emptyMessage: No qualified Energy / Utilities stories found in the last 24 hours.

articles.title: Louisville residents celebrate draft changes for data centers, but wish they came sooner
articles.summary: WHAS11 is reporting on Louisville residents celebrate draft changes for data centers, but wish they came sooner. In plain English, this is a customer-bill and rate-case story: the important details are what regulators approved, how much bills may change, what the utility says the money funds, and what protections or affordability terms are included. For Dustin, the useful read is whether data-center demand is starting to pressure utility forecasts, grid planning, rate design, or public communication around who pays for new infrastructure. This is included in the morning brief because it is dated, source-linked, and matched the scan filters for this section.
articles.emailSummary: A data-center story with possible implications for power demand, grid upgrades, and who pays for capacity.
articles.whyItMatters: Data-center load can reshape utility forecasts, infrastructure plans, rate design, reliability risk, and the analytics needed to separate real demand from speculation.

learningPage.title: What this means
learningPage.explanationText: This is a power-demand story: a large electricity user may change what the utility has to build, forecast, and justify to regulators. The teaching angle is cost allocation. If a data center needs more power or grid upgrades, the important question is whether the company pays or ordinary ratepayers share the bill. The main terms to understand are data center, large load, grid upgrades, ratepayer, utility rates, regulatory review. Read the story by separating three things: the proposed facility, the amount of electricity it may need, and the regulatory/political fight over who pays for infrastructure.

glossary.definition: A large building full of servers. For utilities, it can mean a big new electricity customer that may need grid upgrades.
glossary.whyItMatters: Data centers can consume huge amounts of electricity, so they can change utility forecasts, infrastructure plans, and customer-bill debates.

glossary.definition: A customer or facility that uses enough electricity to affect utility planning, grid equipment, or rates.
glossary.whyItMatters: Large loads matter because one customer can be big enough to change planning assumptions for the whole grid area.

glossary.definition: Physical or operational improvements to the electric system so it can carry and deliver enough power safely.
glossary.whyItMatters: The key question is who benefits from the upgrade and who pays for it.

glossary.definition: A utility customer who pays electric, gas, or water bills.
glossary.whyItMatters: Ratepayer impact is the consumer angle: whether ordinary customers end up funding infrastructure or company costs.

glossary.definition: The prices customers pay for electric, gas, or water service, usually set or reviewed by regulators.
glossary.whyItMatters: Rates are where utility planning, infrastructure costs, and company finances turn into customer impact.

glossary.definition: The process where regulators examine utility plans, costs, reliability claims, and customer impacts.
glossary.whyItMatters: Regulatory review is where private utility decisions become public questions about fairness, reliability, and bills.

articles.summary: GuruFocus is reporting on Insider Sell: John Cornett Sells Shares of PPL Corp. In plain English, this is a utility-operations story: the important details are what changed, which customers or systems are affected, and whether it creates a reliability, regulatory, cost, or data-quality issue. For Dustin, the useful read is whether this changes the local utility story around capacity, reliability, rates, regulation, metering, or the data products leaders need to explain those tradeoffs clearly. This is included in the morning brief because it is dated, source-linked, and matched the scan filters for this section.
